reasoning:
The question essentially asks for the probability that the daily average temperature in Perpignan, France, will be higher on three target dates (Nov 16, Dec 9, Feb 7) relative to Nov 9. I am estimating the probabilities entirely based on local climatology and seasonal progression, since both the reference and target dates reside in the future relative to the October data point, and I have no real-time weather forecasts for November 2025.

In Perpignan, a Mediterranean climate prevails. 
- Nov 9 expected average temperature: ~13.0°C.
- Nov 16 expected temperature: ~11.5°C (Δ ≈ -1.5°C). 
- Dec 9 expected temperature: ~9.0°C (Δ ≈ -4.0°C).
- Feb 7 expected temperature: ~8.5°C (Δ ≈ -4.5°C).

Assuming the standard deviation of daily average temperature differences between any two such dates is approximately 4.5°C to 5.0°C, we can model the probability of the target dates being warmer than Nov 9 using a normal distribution for the difference (Target - Base).
- For Nov 16: P(Diff > 0) ≈ Φ(-1.5 / 4.5) ≈ 0.37 to 0.38 
- For Dec 9: P(Diff > 0) ≈ Φ(-4.0 / 4.8) ≈ 0.20 to 0.21
- For Feb 7: P(Diff > 0) ≈ Φ(-4.5 / 4.8) ≈ 0.17 to 0.18

Although generic forecasting principles recommend drawing long-horizon probabilities closer to 0.5 for non-stationary variables (like stock prices), seasonal weather acts differently: the structural cooling trend through autumn and winter heavily skews the distribution negatively. Therefore, the empirical probabilities fall further below 0.5 as we transition into deep winter.
